---
title: "Visualization"
subtitle: "Introduction to ggplot2"
author: "Ashlyn Barry"
institute: "University of Wisconsin-Madison"
date: 2026-07-08
image: "../../images/thumbnails/image-template.png"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, "../../styles/styleReveal.scss"]
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: auto
    logo: https://brand.ku.edu/sites/brand/files/styles/wide_col_xl_6/public/images/2020/KUMarksLogo.png
    footer: "R for Lifestyle and Brain Health (R-LAB)"
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: true
    code-block-bg: true
    incremental: false
    date-format: long
    embed-resources: true
execute: 
  freeze: auto
---

## What is ggplot2? {.title-slide}

::: {.highlight-box}
**ggplot2 is a popular R package for creating data visualizations**

"Grammar of Graphics" - build visualization by combining independent components
:::

. . .

**Key Strengths:**

- Versatile (scatterplots, boxplots, barcharts...)
- Customizable: easily change formatting, fonts, colors...
- Publication-quality graphics
- Open source and free

## 7 components of creating a chart with ggplot
![](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/articles/ggplot2_files/figure-html/overview_graphic-1.png)

- Essential components: data, mapping, and layer
- Optional components: scales, facets, coordinates, and themes
  - Default modes unless modified directly

## Essential Components

**Data**: Specifies the dataset for the plot
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
ggplot(data = datasetname)
```

**Mapping**: set of instructions on how to "map" data
- In other words, it defines which variables to include and how
- Uses aes() function to define aesthetics of plot
```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
ggplot(data = datasetname, mapping = aes(x = height, y = weight))
```

**Layers**: defines how to display the mapped data
- geom_() function: defines geometry to determine how data is displayed (points, lines, etc.). *More on this soon!*

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
ggplot(
  data = datasetname,
  mapping = aes(x = height, y = weight) +
    geom_point()
)
```

## Getting Started

**Installation Requirements:**

- ggplot2 is a core package of the tidyverse
- tidyverse must be installed and loaded
- You only need to install the package once, but need to load it every time you start a session.

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
install.packages("tidyverse") # install package (once)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
library(tidyverse) # load tidyverse (every new session)
```

::: {.tip-box}
💡 **Tip**: Update R and packages regularly for latest features
:::

## Palmer Penguin Package

::: {.highlight-box}
**Penguins dataset** from the plamerpenguin package contains body measurements of penguins on three islands in the Palmer Archipelago, Antartica. We will be using this dataset to explore different visualizations using ggplot2.
:::

```{r}
#| echo: true
library(palmerpenguins) # load palmer penguins dataset
penguins # load a preview of the data
```

::: {.tip-box}
You may need to run **install.packages("palmerpenguins")** if it wasn't previously loaded on your system.
:::

## Creating a ggplot with penguins

::: {.highlight-box}
What is the relationship between flipper length and body mass index of penguins?
:::

**Step 1:** begin with the function ggplot(), telling it what data to use

**Step 2:** add mapping layer, which defines the aesthetics (layout) of your plot, including defining your x and y axes

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
)
```


## Base plot
```{r}
#| echo: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
)
```

## Geometrical object layer (geom_)

The geom_*() layer defines your plot type. We will create a scatterplot with penguins.


| Plot type |  geom_  |
|-----------|---------|
| Bar chart | geom_bar() |
| Line chart | geom_line() |
| Boxplot | geom_boxplot() |
| Scatterplot | geom_point() |
| Violin plot | geom_violin() |
| Histogram | geom_histogram() |

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point()
```


## Plot data
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point()
```

## Categorizing by species

::: {.highlight-box}
Does the relationship between flipper length and body mass index differ by species?
:::

```{r}
#| echo: true
names(penguins) # view variable names in dataset
```

Modify aesthetics to categorize species by color.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)
) +
  geom_point()
```

## Categorize species by color
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)
) +
  geom_point()
```

## Adding lines of best fit

Next we will ad smooth curves displaying the realtionship between body mass and flipper length by species.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)
) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

# geom_smooth(): geometric object representing data with a smooth line
# method = "lm": line of best fit based on a linear model
```

## Linear models
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)
) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
```

## Single line of best fit

What if we want a single line of best fit, but still want to categorize by species?

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

# color = species in geom_point: only apply color to points
# geom_smooth(method = "lm") now applied to mapping = aes()
```


## Single line of best fit
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
```

## Categorizing by shape

People perceive colors differently, so it is generally not a good idea to categorize only by color. In addition to color, we can also categorize by shapes.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
```

## Categorizing by shape
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
```

## Specify Titles and Axes
We likely do not want the variable name in our dataset to be the title of our axes. We can specify the "labels" of our plot using the labs() function in a new layer. We can also add a title and subtitle in this function.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  )
```

## Clean titles and axes
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  )
```

## Theme Component
Optional component: controls visuals of the plot that are not controlled by the data
- Used to specify look and feel of your plot beyond ggplot default
- Many options, from changing locations of titles and legends to changing the background color

Built-in themes: theme_*() functions

| theme_*() |  Description  |
|-----------|---------------|
| theme_grey() | Default theme: light grey background with white gridlines |
| theme_bw() | Black and white theme with white background and gridlines |
| theme_minimal() | Minimalist: white background and no gridlines |
| theme_classic() | Classic R: white background with solid axes and no gridlines |
| theme_void() | Empty theme: removes all backgrounds, axes, gridlines and labels |

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## theme_minimal()
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Manually modifying theme
::: {.highlight-box}
theme() allows you to manually adjust your theme based your preferences
:::
For example, you can adjust the locations of the title, subtitle, and legend 
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5), # hor. adjust title to center
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5), # hor. adjust subtitle to center
    legend.position = c(0.85, 0.2) # x, y from bottom-left corner on scale 0-1
  )
```

## Manually adjust text location
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5), # hor. adjust to center
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5), # hor. adjust to center
    legend.position = c(0.85, 0.2) # x, y from bottom-left corner on scale 0-1
  )
```

## Remove gridlines
The function element_blank() removes an element entirely. Setting panel.grid.major and panel.grid.minor to element_blank() will remove them from your figure.
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = c(0.85, 0.2),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

## Blank gridlines
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
#| code-line-numbers: 2|3|4
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = c(0.85, 0.2),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

## Black lines identifying axes
What if you don't want gridlines but still want a solid line to signify your x and y axes? You can tell ggplot to draw axes lines using axis.line = element_line().
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = c(0.85, 0.2),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black")
  )
```

## Black lines for axes
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true
ggplot(
  data = penguins,
  mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)
) +
  geom_point(aes(color = species, shape = species)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  labs(
    title = "Body mass and flipper length",
    subtitle = "Dimensions for Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins",
    x = "Flipper length (mm)",
    y = "Body mass (g)",
    color = "Species",
    shape = "Species"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = c(0.85, 0.2),
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.line = element_line(color = "black")
  )
```

## Facets


## Extensions: ggpubr



## Next steps
- Devlop instructions for individual practice time:
  Bring back geometrical object layer (geom_) table layer
  - Have them create a different type of plot (ie, not scatterplot) with penguins
  - ~30 minutes of independent work time, Ashlyn/Brian/Julianne/others available for questions/help
  - ~10-15 minutes of share time
    - Back up: Ashlyn will have another plot created with code that she can walk through if nobody wants to share
